Curriculum → Networking, edge, and request routing → Reverse proxies, gateways, and edge policy

Lesson 4 of 5

Reverse proxies, gateways, and edge policy

Reverse proxies shape inbound traffic by terminating TLS, applying caching and compression, enforcing auth, and routing to internal services.

25-35 min Networking, edge, and request routing 4 guided stops

Study map

What this lesson helps you explain

They let you keep backend services simpler and more focused while centralizing cross-cutting concerns.

Start here Frame the trade-off before diving into architecture. 1. Reverse proxy role Good place for TLS termination, header normalization, and compression2. API gateways and service exposure Useful when clients should not know internal service topology3. When to split concerns CDN: edge caching and global acceleration

Guided walkthrough

Move through the lesson in a clear interview order

4 steps

Start with the framing, walk the concept in order, then use the right rail for prompts, pitfalls, and visual anchors.

1. Interview framing They let you keep backend services simpler and more focused while centralizing cross-cutting concerns. 2. Reverse proxy role Good place for TLS termination, header normalization, and compression3. API gateways and service exposure Useful when clients should not know internal service topology4. When to split concerns CDN: edge caching and global acceleration
1

Start with this framing

Interview framing

They let you keep backend services simpler and more focused while centralizing cross-cutting concerns.

What to say first

  • Differentiate reverse proxy, gateway, CDN, and load balancer.
  • Use the proxy tier for shared cross-cutting behavior.
  • Keep business logic out of the gateway.
2

Study section

Reverse proxy role

A reverse proxy sits in front of application servers, unlike a forward proxy that acts on behalf of clients.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Good place for TLS termination, header normalization, and compression
  • Can cache shared responses close to the application tier
  • Often paired with WAF or bot controls
3

Study section

API gateways and service exposure

Gateways aggregate routing, authentication, request shaping, and sometimes schema translation for internal microservices.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Useful when clients should not know internal service topology
  • Keep gateway logic thin to avoid creating a monolith choke point
  • Prefer clear ownership of shared policies
4

Study section

When to split concerns

CDN, load balancer, reverse proxy, and gateway can coexist. Explain what each layer owns instead of collapsing them into one vague box.

Call these points out explicitly

  • CDN: edge caching and global acceleration
  • Load balancer: healthy distribution
  • Gateway/proxy: policy, routing, and internal exposure control

Reference diagram

Visual anchor

Reverse proxies centralize policy and hide internal topology from clients.

Adapted from The System Design Primer by Donne Martin (CC BY 4.0).

Checklist

Things to say clearly

  • Differentiate reverse proxy, gateway, CDN, and load balancer.
  • Use the proxy tier for shared cross-cutting behavior.
  • Keep business logic out of the gateway.
  • Discuss observability and auth policies at the edge.

Common pitfalls

What to avoid

  • Calling every front-door component a load balancer.
  • Stuffing product-specific orchestration into the gateway.
  • Ignoring hop-by-hop headers, compression cost, or auth propagation.

Interview prompts

Practice aloud

  • What belongs in an API gateway versus inside a service?
  • How would you roll out a new auth policy safely?
  • When should a reverse proxy cache responses?

Related topics

Keep the thread going

Load balancing Networking, edge, and request routingAPI design: REST, RPC, and contracts Application architecture and APIs

Simulation lab

Simulation lab: Reverse proxies, gateways, and edge policy

Watch the edge, cache, and origin layers react as traffic shape changes across regions and protection controls. Apply it to reverse proxies, gateways, and edge policy so the trade-offs feel concrete.

Use this lab to turn "Reverse proxies, gateways, and edge policy" into a measurable system instead of a static definition.Pressure the path around reverse proxy role and watch how it interacts with api gateways and service exposure.They let you keep backend services simpler and more focused while centralizing cross-cutting concerns.

Choose an API path

Which flow are you stressing?

Focus metrics

  • CDN hit ratio
  • origin utilization
  • edge p95
  • policy saturation

Workload profile

Traffic shape

Exercise workspace

Multi-file topology and override editor

8 nodes

Use file tabs, code completion, inline previews, and diagnostics to tune the topology and scripted overrides without leaving the browser.

topology.flow Edit the topology and override script with quick inserts, inline diagnostics, and visible structure signals.

Current file summary

8 nodes · 7 links

Signals

What the editor found

  • Line 1: edge · 120000 rps · 5 ms
  • Line 2: edge · 95000 rps · 8 ms
  • Line 3: cache · 140000 rps · 3 ms
  • Line 4: service · 42000 rps · 10 ms
  • Line 5: service · 22000 rps · 18 ms
  • Line 6: service · 50000 rps · 6 ms

Built-in runtime adapters

Browser and WebAssembly execution targets

No eval

The simulator runs its DSL interpreters in-browser today and now exposes adapter slots for WebAssembly-backed runtimes like Pyodide or custom interpreters.

Native flow-graph compiler

Runs the topology and simulation DSL interpreters directly in the browser.

Available now
Pyodide / Python WASM adapter

Ready for browser-side Python interpreters compiled to WebAssembly.

Register adapter
Lua / custom WASM adapter

Use this slot for additional in-browser interpreters backed by WebAssembly modules.

Register adapter

Good first experiments

  • Differentiate reverse proxy, gateway, CDN, and load balancer.
  • Use the proxy tier for shared cross-cutting behavior.
  • Keep business logic out of the gateway.

Interactive diagram

Compiled simulation topology

Rendering diagram…

This preview is generated from the current flow-graph input.

API modeling notes

How this simulator thinks

  • Start with the critical path from Reverse proxy role and use the metrics to explain where latency or capacity moves.
  • Use the simulator to justify checklist items like Differentiate reverse proxy, gateway, CDN, and load balancer. before you propose scaling changes.
  • Treat the async queue and worker path as the escape hatch for ideas from API gateways and service exposure.

What you get after a run

Simulation output

  • Per-node utilization, queue depth, and dropped traffic
  • Average, p95, and p99 latency for the selected API path
  • Probable fixes mapped to the dominant bottleneck

Interactive practice lab

Write, save, and move through the mock interview

Answers stay in your browser so you can come back later without needing automated grading.

0/3 prompts saved Prompt 1 of 3

Prompt flow

What you will practice

interview

Explain the concept

Not saved yet

Teach "Reverse proxies, gateways, and edge policy" back to an interviewer. Summarize what it is, when you would use it, and why it matters in a real system.

Goal for this step

Explain the topic in interviewer-friendly language before diving into implementation details.

Use these guardrails

  • What belongs in an API gateway versus inside a service?
  • Reverse proxy role
  • API gateways and service exposure
  • Differentiate reverse proxy, gateway, CDN, and load balancer.

Suggested structure

  • Definition
  • When to use it
  • Benefits
  • Trade-offs

Bring your own model
